rules: TOK-1 (Confined type formation). A struct or enum type may be declared `confined`; a confined type is region-parameterized and affine, and its fields may be borrow types (&'p T or &uniq 'p T at a declared region parameter 'p), confined types instantiated at declared region parameters, or ordinary owned non-confined types. Only confined types may contain borrow-typed or confined-typed fields; a non-confined type declaration containing either is ill-formed. Sealed-form token, guard, and cursor result types are opaque confined types declared in the normative form table; user code cannot construct opaque confined values by literal.

TOK-2 (Confinement of placement). A confined value may exist only as a local binding, a call argument, a call result, or a match scrutinee or arm binding. It cannot be stored into any heap allocation, into any sealed-form container (instantiating a sealed form's element, key, or value type parameter with a confined type is ill-formed), or into any field of a non-confined aggregate; there is no other place a confined value can go.

TOK-3 (Region instantiation and escape). When a confined value is created with region parameter 'p tied to source binding C (per LOAN-2), 'p is instantiated as C's declared region if C is a reference binding, else as the region of C's enclosing block. A confined binding must not outlive its instantiated region. A function may return a confined value only at a region that is one of its caller-supplied region parameters; returning a confined value whose region is tied to a callee-local block is rejected.

TOK-4 (Confined movement). Confined values move as affine values between local bindings; a move renames the value's held-loan entries to the destination binding. The destination must be a fresh binding or an existing binding of identical type whose value has been consumed; assigning over a live confined binding is rejected. A `&` (shared) borrow of a confined binding is statement-scoped and transfers no loans; there is no way to duplicate a confined value.

LOAN-1 (Loan state and update discipline). The checker keeps, per live binding, a loan set — a finite multiset of entries (holder binding, strength in {shared, unique}) — and, per confined binding, a source map (source binding -> strength). Both change only at confined-value creation (LOAN-2), consumption (LOAN-5), moves (TOK-4), and scope end; each statement is processed exactly once, in order: legality checks (LOAN-3, LOAN-4, LOAN-6, LOAN-7), then consumes, then issues. At function entry, each confined parameter holds a loan on each borrow-mode parameter its region parameters tie it to (LOAN-2 unification), with that parameter's mode as strength; no other analysis updates loan state.

LOAN-2 (Issue). A statement that creates a confined value V issues loans held by V's binding: (a) a confined literal with borrow-typed fields issues, per field, a loan on the borrowed source binding with the field's mode as strength, the borrow being minted in the literal statement itself; (b) a call whose result type is confined issues, for each region parameter 'p of the result type, a loan on the argument bound to the unique borrow-mode parameter of the callee whose declared region is 'p — strength unique if that parameter's mode is &uniq, shared if & — recording that argument binding in V's source map. If 'p occurs on zero or more than one borrow-mode parameter of the callee signature, the signature is rejected at declaration. Sealed-form ops declare signatures in the same language and are covered by (b).

LOAN-3 (Unique exclusion). While a binding C's loan set contains a unique entry held by token t, every statement that operates on C — any call taking a borrow of C, any borrow minting on C, any new issue on C, any move, own, or drop of C — is rejected, with one exception: a single call that consumes t by own may also take borrows of C as arguments in that same statement. Uses of t itself are not restricted by this rule.

LOAN-4 (Shared restriction). While C's loan set is nonempty and all entries are shared, statements taking only `&` (shared) borrows of C — including further shared issues on C — are permitted; any statement taking &uniq or own of C, any unique issue on C, and any mutation of C is rejected.

LOAN-5 (Consume). A call argument of mode own with confined type consumes the token: its binding becomes consumed and every loan it holds is removed, after the statement's legality checks and before the statement's issues; a call may therefore consume a token and, via LOAN-2 on its result, issue fresh loans in the same statement (consume-and-reissue). At scope end a still-live confined binding is auto-consumed by the compiler-derived drop (STOR-3), discharging its drop obligation (unlock, cursor release, endpoint retire) and removing its loans; block-end drops run in reverse binding order, so same-block tokens are consumed before their sources are dropped.

LOAN-6 (Loaned sources are pinned). A binding whose loan set is nonempty cannot be moved, passed by own, explicitly dropped, or rebound; until its loan set empties it may be used only as LOAN-3 and LOAN-4 permit.

LOAN-7 (Instance brands). Every loan and source-map entry records the source's binding identity; distinct bindings are distinct brands, independent of type and region equality. A call that takes a borrow-mode argument B at declared region 'p and also takes (by own or by borrow) a confined argument whose type carries 'p requires that confined value's source map to contain exactly B at 'p; any mismatch is rejected.

LOAN-8 (Join agreement). Every match arm is checked from the same entry loan state and must end in an identical loan state — the same loan sets, source maps, and consumed-flags for every binding live at the join; a loop body must end in exactly the loan state it began in, and every break statement must occur at that same state, which is the loop's exit state. Any disagreement is rejected.

derivations.derivation: S1 `let s = seq.new()`: result not confined, no issue [LOAN-2 n/a]; L(s)={}. S2 `s.push(1)`: legality — L(s)={} so LOAN-3/4/6 impose nothing; statement-scoped &uniq mint on s per existing T-A minting; result unit, no issue; L(s)={}. S3 `s.push(2)`: identical; L(s)={}. Scope end: no live tokens, drop s [STOR-3]. The loan judgment is conservative over token-free programs: statement-scoped minting composes unchanged.

S1: L(t)={}. S2 `let e = t.entry(k)`: result type entry<'t> is confined; 't occurs on exactly one borrow-mode parameter (&uniq t) [LOAN-2b] -> issue unique loan on t held by e; region 't := t's block region [TOK-3]; L(t)={unique:e}, src(e)={t:unique}. S3 `t.insert(k2,v2)`: statement takes &uniq borrow of t while L(t) contains a unique entry held by e, and the statement does not consume e -> REJECT [LOAN-3]. (Had S3 been absent, S4 would consume e [LOAN-5], emptying L(t).) The insert could rehash and dangle e's interior pointer; LOAN-3 makes the interleaving unrepresentable.

S1: L(t)={}. S2 `let c = t.range(lo,hi)`: confined result, 't on exactly one borrow-mode parameter (& t) -> issue shared loan [LOAN-2b]; L(t)={shared:c}, src(c)={t:shared}. Loop entry state: L(t)={shared:c}, c live. S3 `let n = c.next()`: an op on binding c itself — c carries no loans against it, statement-scoped &uniq mint on c is legal; result not confined, no issue; state unchanged. S4 match, Some arm: `t.remove(kv.k)` takes &uniq borrow of t while L(t) is nonempty all-shared -> REJECT [LOAN-4]. Checking stops at the first rejection; the None arm (break at loop-entry state) is irrelevant. A remove while the cursor lives could shrink or rehash the table under the cursor.

S1 `let g = m.lock()`: confined result guard<'m>; 'm occurs on exactly one borrow-mode parameter -> issue on m [LOAN-2b], strength shared (parameter mode &); 'm := 'a, m's declared region [TOK-3]; L(m)={shared:g}, src(g)={m:shared}. Loop entry state S: L(m)={shared:g}, g live. S2 `predicate(&g)`: statement-scoped shared borrow of the token binding g [TOK-4]; no loan change. S3 match: Done arm: `break` at state S = loop-exit state [LOAN-8 ok]. Wait arm: `let g2 = cv.wait(m, own g)`: legality — takes & of m while L(m) all-shared, permitted [LOAN-4]; consume g -> L(m)={}, g consumed [LOAN-5]; issue — result guard<'m>, 'm ties to the single borrow-mode parameter bound to m -> L(m)={shared:g2}, src(g2)={m:shared} [LOAN-2b]; brand check: consumed guard's src map {m} matches borrow argument m at 'm [LOAN-7 ok]. S4 `g = g2`: destination g is consumed and of identical type -> move, rename holder [TOK-4]; L(m)={shared:g}, src(g)={m:shared}. Arm exit = S; both arms and the loop bottom equal loop top [LOAN-8 ok]. After loop: state S; scope end: reverse-order drop auto-consumes g -> unlock, L(m)={} [LOAN-5]; parameters m, cv have no drops.

S1, S2: L(a)={}, L(b)={}. S3 `let pa = a.producer()`: issue shared on a [LOAN-2b]; L(a)={shared:pa}, src(pa)={a:shared}. S4 `let cb = b.consumer()`: issue shared on b; L(b)={shared:cb}, src(cb)={b:shared}. S5 `let pa2 = b.push(own pa, x)`: the call takes borrow-mode argument b at declared region 'r and confined argument pa whose type carries 'r; LOAN-7 requires src(pa) to contain exactly b at 'r, but src(pa)={a} and a, b are distinct bindings, hence distinct brands -> REJECT [LOAN-7]. Region equality cannot rescue it: both rings live in the same block region, but brands are binding identities, not regions. The symmetric swap (a.push(own cb,...)) rejects identically.

S1 `let env = Env { t: &tbl }`: confined literal with one shared borrow field minted in the literal statement -> issue shared loan on tbl held by env [LOAN-2a]; 'e := 'a, tbl's declared region [TOK-3]; L(tbl)={shared:env}, src(env)={tbl:shared}. Between S1 and S2 any &uniq/own op on tbl would reject [LOAN-4] — the loan spans the window in which env carries interior access. S2 `par.for_chunks(data, own env, body)`: legality — takes &uniq of data, L(data)={} ok; takes & of tbl? no, tbl is reached only through env inside the trusted form; env is moved by own — env's own loan set (loans against env) is empty, so LOAN-6 does not fire; consume env -> L(tbl)={} after the statement [LOAN-5]; result unit, no issue. The sealed form's contract: it splits data into disjoint &uniq chunks, passes env to bodies by statement-internal shared borrow, and joins every body before returning; body's row reads('e) writes('c) is checked syntactically. S3 `data.set(0,9)`: L(data)={}, ordinary &uniq op, legal. Scope end: nothing live.

soundness_sketch: Invariant I: at every program point, for every live confined value V and every source S in src(V), S's loan set contains an entry (V, strength). I is established at every creation path — confined literals expose their borrow fields syntactically (LOAN-2a), calls returning confined types are forced through region-parameter unification with exactly one borrow-mode parameter (LOAN-2b), sealed-form ops declare signatures in the same language, and function entry installs signature-implied loans (LOAN-1) — and it cannot be silently lost: TOK-1/TOK-2 make every untracked placement (heap, sealed containers, non-confined aggregates) unrepresentable, so every value carrying interior access is a tracked local binding; TOK-4 renames rather than drops entries on moves; LOAN-5 removes entries only when the token is consumed; LOAN-8 forbids any join where arms disagree about I's bookkeeping. Given I, exclusion: every channel that could mutate, move, drop, or reallocate S while V lives is rejected — &uniq/own ops and unique issues under LOAN-3 (unique) and LOAN-4 (shared), moves/owns/drops under LOAN-6 — except the single statement that consumes the unique holder, and within that statement single-threaded program order makes the call atomic: the callee holds V only for the duration of the call, is checked under the same rules with signature-implied loans, and can extend access past the call only by returning a confined result, at which point LOAN-2 reinstates a caller-side loan in the same statement, leaving no gap where interior access exists and S is unloaned in any frame that can reach S. Lexical containment: TOK-3 pins V's region to S's own region or block, so V's binding cannot outlive S, scope-end auto-consume is always reached, and STOR-3 reverse drop order consumes same-block tokens before their sources; returns can carry V only upward at caller-supplied regions, i.e. only to frames where the source is a parameter the caller still holds loaned. Completeness of per-binding tracking rests on the kernel's existing shape: single ownership, OWN-5 resolved-place exclusivity, and the absence of storable borrows mean binding identity is the only aliasing channel to S, so lexical per-binding loan sets miss no alias — this is precisely why no dataflow is needed where Rust needs regionck. Brands (LOAN-7) prevent the remaining confusion channel, using a token minted from one instance to reach a sibling's interior. Across the scoped-par boundary: the par sealed form is the only concurrency source; every borrow a parallel body can reach is a field of a confined env whose sources are loaned from env's creation until the par statement consumes it, so the loans span the entire parallel execution window; the trusted form joins all bodies before its statement completes (no borrow outlives the statement), splits the &uniq buffer into disjoint chunks internally, and body effect rows are checked syntactically — so no body can observe a source event, and no source event can be interleaved with a body. Hence in any accepted program, every mutation, move, or reallocation of a source is ordered strictly after the consume of every token carrying interior access into it: no token can dangle.

adversarial_corpus.name: A1-escape-by-return
adversarial_corpus.why: TOK-3: e's region is tied to the callee-local block of t, not to a caller-supplied region parameter; the return is rejected, so a token can never outlive its source's frame.

adversarial_corpus.name: A2-token-in-struct
adversarial_corpus.why: TOK-1: a non-confined type with a confined field is ill-formed at declaration; the value-level store never typechecks. The heap variant (conc_queue<entry<'h>>) is rejected by TOK-2's sealed-form type-parameter ban.

adversarial_corpus.name: A3-two-unique-tokens
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-3: while the unique loan held by e1 lives, any new issue on t is rejected and the statement does not consume e1. Disjoint-key sessions are deliberately sacrificed for whole-binding decidability; the escape is a fatter form op.

adversarial_corpus.name: A4-two-shared-cursors
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-4: all-shared loan sets admit further shared issues and shared ops; L(t)={shared:c1, shared:c2} during the reads. Both cursors auto-consume at scope end. Confirms the shared tier is not vacuously strict.

adversarial_corpus.name: A5-shared-then-unique-upgrade
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-4: a unique issue (an &uniq-mode op on t) while L(t) is nonempty all-shared is rejected. There is no upgrade path; the cursor must be consumed first.

adversarial_corpus.name: A6-move-while-loaned
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-6: t's loan set is nonempty, so move/own/drop/rebind of t is rejected. Otherwise c's interior pointer would outlive t's storage under the new name.

adversarial_corpus.name: A7-arm-imbalanced-consume
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-8: the T arm ends with g consumed and L(m)={}, the F arm with g live and L(m)={shared:g}; the join states differ, so the program is rejected rather than reasoned about path-sensitively. The blessed shape consumes in both arms or in neither.

adversarial_corpus.name: A8-helper-issue-and-reuse
adversarial_corpus.why: The hard-constraint helper idiom: inside reserve, LOAN-2b issues on parameter t and TOK-3 permits the return at caller-supplied 't; at the call site LOAN-2b re-derives the unique loan on main's t purely from the signature. No flow analysis, one blessed writing for wrapped writers.

adversarial_corpus.name: A9-preloop-token-consumed-in-body
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-8: loop-body exit state (e consumed, L(t)={}) differs from entry state (e live, L(t)={unique:e}); rejected without simulating a second iteration — which would have been a use-after-consume. The accepted variants are A10-style per-iteration tokens or P4-style consume-and-reissue into the same binding.

adversarial_corpus.name: A10-per-iteration-token
adversarial_corpus.why: Loop-carried balance: the body enters and exits with L(t)={} (issue then consume inside the body; scope end would auto-consume anyway), so LOAN-8's entry-equals-exit equality holds and one body check suffices.

adversarial_corpus.name: A11-cross-source-reissue (hard call)
adversarial_corpus.why: Hard call: it looks like brand laundering, but it is honest issue/consume composition — LOAN-5 ends the loan on a, LOAN-2b independently issues on b because the result's region 'b unifies with the single borrow-mode parameter y, and the callee cannot forge producer<'b> except through y's own trusted ops (opaque confined types, TOK-1). Accepting this is the design intent; if hostile review finds a forgery path inside any form, this is the first program to flip.

adversarial_corpus.name: A12-ambiguous-region-signature (hard call)
adversarial_corpus.why: Hard call: each arm is locally sound, and a flow-sensitive checker could accept by loaning both x and y at the call site. LOAN-2b instead rejects the signature at declaration because result region 't occurs on two borrow-mode parameters, so the call-site source (and brand) would be ambiguous. This is reject-when-unsure made concrete: the conservative alternative (loan both) was declined to keep source maps singleton-exact and LOAN-7 brands decidable.

decidability: The checker carries one lexical environment: for each live binding, its type, a consumed flag, the loan multiset held against it, and — if confined — its source map; plus the block stack. Each statement is visited exactly once in program order and dispatched by syntactic form (let-literal, let-call, call, assign/move, match, loop, break, scope end): the issue/consume effects of a call are computed from the callee signature by first-order matching of named region parameters against argument bindings — a table lookup with no search, since LOAN-2 already rejected at declaration any signature where a result region parameter occurs on other than exactly one borrow-mode parameter — then the legality predicates (LOAN-3/4/6/7) run as set-membership tests on the current environment, then consumes are applied, then issues. Match arms are each checked once from a copy of the entry environment and their exit environments compared for byte equality (LOAN-8); loops check the body once and compare exit to entry — equality replaces any fixpoint, because a body that maps the state to itself does so on every iteration, and a body that does not is rejected; every break is compared against the loop-exit state. The pass is single, syntax-directed, and O(program size x live bindings); there is no dataflow lattice, no path sensitivity, and no backtracking, because every ambiguity (multi-occurrence regions, divergent joins, unknown source) rejects instead of branching.
honest_risks: Whole-binding unique loans are coarse: two live entry tokens into provably disjoint keys, or a cursor plus an entry on one table, are rejected (LOAN-3/LOAN-4). Real Hashbrown and SQLite code wants multi-token sessions; the only escape is fatter form-table ops (e.g., a two-key entry op), which grows exactly the surface the minimality doctrine wants small. This is the most likely too-restrictive point against the fourteen frozen operations.

LOAN-2b's one-borrow-parameter-per-region rule rejects honest signatures where one region genuinely annotates two arguments (compare two cursors from one table, table-plus-auxiliary-view helpers, A12's pick). Writers must split region parameters even when every call site instantiates them equally, and some shapes have no split that typechecks; blessed-pattern coverage for wrapped idioms may hit this wall.

LOAN-8's exact-equality joins reject harmless imbalance and force explicit consume statements in non-consuming arms (A7). It is deliberate reject-when-unsure, but it puts a new obligation on generated code, and PATTERNS would need a blessed arm-balancing shape or writers will emit near-misses at scale.

Brand equals binding identity, which is coarser than instance identity through views: a token minted from a view binding into a container-of-containers brands against the view, so re-viewing the same inner instance yields a token unusable against the new view binding. Per-instance runtime brands would fix this but leave the purely lexical fragment; for now nested-container token idioms are effectively unwritable.

TOK-3 instantiates a token's region at the source's own region (not the issue statement), trusting the loan — not the region — to carry exclusion. That is sound only while sealed-form opacity and the no-stored-borrows rule stay absolute: any future channel that can invalidate a source binding's interior without a statement on that binding (e.g., a form op on a sibling binding sharing internal storage, or a user-constructible opaque token) breaks the invariant. A11 is the canary; fact-channel-grade hostile review of every form-table signature is load-bearing, and a green checker is not that review.

Guards take shared-strength loans on mutexes (mutexes must stay shareable across par bodies), so a same-thread second m.lock() typechecks and deadlocks at runtime. Classified as liveness-not-safety, consistent with the D1 bug-class list, but it will surprise, and flipping guards to unique strength would need the LOAN-3 consume-exception for wait and would forbid sharing the mutex binding at all while locked — a real design fork left open.

The LOAN-3 consume-exception assumes at most one token is consumed per statement against a uniquely-loaned source; ANF makes multi-consume statements unlikely but form ops taking two own tokens plus a borrow of one token's source would need re-review before being declared. The rule text should be read as single-exception until then.

Function-entry signature-implied loans (LOAN-1) mean a callee trusts the declared tying; the loan judgment is disjoint from effect rows, so a signature can be loan-honest but row-dishonest or vice versa. Rows are checked syntactically as before, but the interaction (e.g., an issuing op with an empty writes row enabling optimizer facts) is a new fact channel and must go through hostile adversarial review before any optimizer consumes loan facts.
